seams.trust_assumption: A service provider trusts a SAML/OIDC assertion that is signed and names its own entity, treating the Audience/Recipient and InResponseTo fields as a faithful binding to the request it actually issued.
seams.violation: An attacker forges or replays an assertion whose signature wrapping, comment-injected NameID, or mismatched audience still passes the SP's relaxed validation, letting a token minted for one party or one request be accepted as authentication for another.

techniques.name: SAML signature wrapping (XSW) and comment-injection NameID confusion
OIDC audience/azp spoof and token reuse across relying parties

seams.trust_assumption: An IdP trusts that an approved MFA push reflects the legitimate user's deliberate consent at that moment, treating the tap as proof the user intended to authorize the session that triggered it.
seams.violation: Holding a valid password, the attacker repeatedly triggers push prompts until a worn-down or confused user approves one, so a momentary tap stands in for intent and the IdP completes a login the user never started.

techniques.name: MFA push bombing / prompt fatigue to harvest approval

seams.trust_assumption: The IdP trusts that a device-code or primary-refresh-token flow is being completed on the same enrolled device and by the same user that initiated it, treating possession of the code or PRT as proof of device-bound provenance.
seams.violation: An attacker initiates the flow and tricks the user into authenticating the attacker's pending code, or extracts a PRT and its session key from a compromised host, replaying device-bound tokens to mint sessions from elsewhere.

techniques.name: Device-code authentication phishing
Primary Refresh Token theft and session-key replay (ROADtoken, pass-the-PRT)

seams.trust_assumption: A tenant trusts assertions from any external IdP or federation realm it has added to its trust configuration, accepting that the foreign issuer authenticated and authorized the subject it vouches for.
seams.violation: An attacker who controls or has tampered with a federated trust (added a rogue domain, forged a Golden SAML token with the stolen token-signing key, or abused B2B/guest federation) mints assertions a trusting tenant accepts as fully authenticated identities.

techniques.name: Golden SAML forged token-signing key abuse
Rogue federated domain / cross-tenant trust addition

seams.trust_assumption: A CI runner trusts the pipeline definition and the scripts it references in the repository as authoritative build steps, executing whatever the checked-out config tells it to run with the pipeline's privileges.
seams.violation: An attacker who can influence the pipeline file or a referenced script (direct push, a pull_request build that runs untrusted code, or an injectable build parameter) turns repo-supplied data into runner-side command execution.

techniques.name: Poisoned Pipeline Execution via controllable CI config/scripts (D-PPE/I-PPE)
Workflow command / build-parameter injection in CI logs

seams.trust_assumption: A cloud provider trusts the short-lived OIDC token a CI runner presents as proof that the named workflow on the named branch is requesting access, and the runner trusts in-process steps to see only their own secrets.
seams.violation: Code running in the build reads injected secrets and the runner's OIDC/identity token from environment or the token endpoint and exfiltrates them, assuming a workflow's machine identity to reach the cloud control plane from outside the pipeline.

techniques.name: CI secret and environment exfiltration from build step
OIDC id-token theft / overbroad federated subject claim abuse

seams.trust_assumption: A pipeline trusts a self-hosted runner and the build caches it restores to be clean and dedicated, treating cached dependencies and a persistent runner's prior state as untainted inputs inherited into every job.
seams.violation: An attacker who lands one job on a non-ephemeral runner or who can write to a shared cache key plants tooling, dependencies, or state that later jobs and other repositories silently inherit and execute with their own trust.

techniques.name: Self-hosted runner persistence and cross-job contamination
Build cache poisoning across jobs/repositories

seams.trust_assumption: A deployer trusts that a mutable artifact tag (a container :latest, a moving release tag, a registry repository name) still points at the reviewed, signed build it pulled before, treating the human-readable label as a stable identity.
seams.violation: An attacker with push access repoints or overwrites a tag, or wins a dependency-confusion/typosquat name race, so consumers resolving the same label pull a swapped image whose digest never matched what was approved.

techniques.name: Registry tag mutation / image overwrite (mutable tags)
Dependency confusion and registry typosquatting

seams.trust_assumption: An API trusts a client-supplied object identifier as authorization, assuming that because the caller knows or was handed an object id, the caller is entitled to act on that object.
seams.violation: An attacker enumerates or substitutes object ids (BOLA/IDOR) and reaches records belonging to other users or tenants, because the endpoint authenticates the caller but never checks that this caller owns the referenced object.

techniques.name: Broken Object Level Authorization / IDOR enumeration

seams.trust_assumption: An API trusts that the request body and parameter set contain only the fields the client is meant to set, binding incoming keys directly onto internal objects and assuming the parser's last-wins or array handling matches the security layer's.
seams.violation: An attacker adds privileged attributes (role, isAdmin, owner) the UI never exposes, or duplicates/pollutes parameters so the auth check and the business logic read different values, smuggling state changes past validation.

techniques.name: Mass assignment / auto-binding of unexpected fields
HTTP parameter pollution and parser-disagreement bypass

seams.trust_assumption: A JWT verifier trusts the token's own alg header to choose the verification key and algorithm, letting the attacker-controlled token dictate how its own signature will be checked.
seams.violation: An attacker sets alg to none, or flips RS256 to HS256 so the public key is used as an HMAC secret, or swaps the kid to a key they control, producing a token the verifier validates as authentically signed.

techniques.name: JWT alg=none / RS256-to-HS256 key confusion / kid injection

seams.trust_assumption: An API trusts that a multi-step workflow advances in order and that its rate counters observe every attempt, assuming clients reach each state only through the prior, validated step and at the throttled pace.
seams.violation: An attacker replays, races, or skips directly to a later endpoint, or splits requests across keys/casing to dodge counters, so a guard that was true at one step is never re-checked when state is acted on or limits are evaded.

techniques.name: Workflow / state-machine step skipping and forced browsing
Rate-limit and anti-automation bypass via key/race manipulation

seams.trust_assumption: A receiving mail system and its readers trust SPF/DKIM/DMARC alignment and the visible From header as proof of sender identity, assuming envelope and header fields that pass policy belong to the claimed organization.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits permissive or missing DMARC, header/display-name mismatch, or a confusable lookalike domain so a forged or impersonating message passes authentication checks and presents as a trusted sender to the recipient.

techniques.name: SPF/DKIM/DMARC spoofing and header-from alignment abuse
Lookalike / IDN homograph domain impersonation

seams.trust_assumption: A cloud collaboration suite trusts a user-consented OAuth application's granted scopes as a durable, non-interactive grant, and trusts user-defined mailbox rules as the account owner's standing intent independent of any login.
seams.violation: An attacker phishes consent for a malicious app (illicit consent grant) or plants forwarding/hiding inbox rules, establishing token-backed mailbox access and silent exfiltration that survive password resets and need no re-authentication.

techniques.name: Illicit OAuth consent-grant application persistence
Malicious mailbox / inbox forwarding rule persistence

seams.trust_assumption: A collaboration platform trusts that messages, files, and links arriving inside a tenant workspace come from vetted internal participants, extending in-platform familiarity to external guests and federated tenants.
seams.violation: An attacker abuses guest access, external federation, or a permissive sharing link to deliver payloads and pretexts inside the trusted Teams/Slack surface, where the platform's perceived-internal context lowers the recipient's guard.

techniques.name: Teams/Slack external-guest and tenant-federation phishing
Open sharing-link / anonymous-access file delivery

seams.trust_assumption: A verifier trusts that a valid digital signature or matching hash uniquely binds to the exact content it was computed over, assuming no second input can produce the same digest or satisfy the same signature.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits a broken or downgradable primitive (MD5/SHA-1 chosen-prefix collision, length-extension, or a malleable signature) to craft a second document or token that verifies against a signature or hash issued for benign content.

techniques.name: Hash-collision and chosen-prefix forgery (MD5/SHA-1, length-extension)
Signature malleability / verification bypass

seams.trust_assumption: A relying party trusts any certificate that chains to a root in its trust store, regardless of how that leaf was issued, equating a valid chain with authority over the named identity.
seams.violation: An attacker who obtains a mis-issued or fraudulent certificate, abuses a weak ACME/domain-validation path, or plants a rogue root impersonates the certified identity and is accepted by every party trusting the chain.

techniques.name: Certificate mis-issuance and domain-validation abuse
Rogue / installed root-CA trust injection

seams.trust_assumption: A cryptosystem trusts that ciphertext reveals nothing about plaintext, assuming error responses are uniform and that each encryption uses a unique IV/nonce so structure never leaks across messages.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits distinguishable padding/decrypt errors or a repeated IV/nonce to recover or forge plaintext byte by byte, turning an oracle or keystream reuse into a format-boundary leak the design assumed was sealed.

techniques.name: Padding-oracle plaintext recovery and CBC bit-flipping
IV/nonce reuse keystream recovery (CTR/GCM, ECB patterning)

seams.trust_assumption: Two endpoints trust the negotiated protocol version and ciphersuite to be the strongest both support, assuming the handshake messages that select it cannot be tampered with by the path between them.
seams.violation: A man-in-the-middle strips, blocks, or rewrites negotiation so the parties settle on an obsolete version or weak/export cipher, rolling back to a primitive whose break lets the attacker read or forge the now-degraded session.

techniques.name: TLS/SSH version and ciphersuite downgrade (FREAK, Logjam, POODLE-class)

seams.trust_assumption: A cross-account IAM role trusts that any principal presenting the agreed external-id shared secret in its AssumeRole call is the intended third party, so a role with a missing, blank, or guessable external-id is treated as if only the partner could invoke it.
seams.violation: An attacker in an unrelated account assumes a role whose trust policy omits or uses a predictable external-id, becoming a confused deputy that wields the target account's permissions without ever compromising a credential.

techniques.name: AssumeRole external-id confused-deputy (missing/guessable external id, wildcard principal trust policy)
Cross-tenant role/identity-federation chaining across vendor SaaS integrations

seams.trust_assumption: An object store, disk snapshot, or machine-image registry trusts that whatever ACL or sharing scope is set is the owner's deliberate intent, so a bucket left public, a snapshot shared with all-accounts, or an AMI marked public is served as authorized distribution rather than misconfiguration.
seams.violation: An attacker enumerates and reads world-readable buckets, public EBS/RDS snapshots, and public AMIs, harvesting credentials, source, and data from stores whose permissive defaults were mistaken for intentional sharing.

techniques.name: Public object-store enumeration and download (S3/GCS/Azure Blob anonymous or all-users ACL)
Public snapshot / shared-AMI plunder for embedded secrets and data

seams.trust_assumption: A serverless function trusts that the event record delivered by its event source (S3 notification, queue message, API Gateway request, DynamoDB stream) is inert data describing what happened, not attacker-controlled instructions, so attacker-influenced fields flow unsanitized into queries, command lines, and downstream calls.
seams.violation: An attacker who can write to the event source plants payloads in object keys, message bodies, or request fields that the function interprets as control — injecting commands, NoSQL/SQL, or SSRF — turning a passive trigger into code execution under the function's role.

techniques.name: Event-source data-as-control injection (S3/SQS/SNS/API-Gateway event field into eval/query/exec)
Function event-payload poisoning leading to privilege use of the execution role

seams.trust_assumption: The cloud control-plane API trusts a valid long-lived access-key / secret pair as conclusive proof of the principal's identity regardless of where, when, or how it is used, so a leaked static key authenticates an attacker exactly as it would the owner with no session, device, or origin binding.
seams.violation: An attacker who recovers a static access key from code, CI logs, dotfiles, or a phished console session calls the control plane directly, persisting and escalating because the key carries no contextual binding the operator could not replicate.

techniques.name: Long-lived access-key abuse (leaked AWS keys / GCP SA keys / Azure SP secrets used from attacker host)
Key-based privilege enumeration and IAM self-escalation (PassRole / policy attach / version set)

seams.trust_assumption: Cloud detection trusts that the trail of control-plane events it receives is complete and authoritative — that CloudTrail/GuardDuty/Activity-Log coverage reflects all activity — so a principal with logging-admin rights can shrink the observable timeline by disabling, redirecting, or filtering the very source the SOC reasons over.
seams.violation: An attacker with config permissions stops trails, deletes log buckets, removes regions from coverage, or suspends GuardDuty/Defender detectors, so subsequent actions occur inside a manufactured blind spot the SOC cannot see because it trusts the feed as the ground truth.

techniques.name: Cloud-trail / activity-log disable, delete, or selective filter
Threat-detector (GuardDuty/Defender/SCC) suspension or finding suppression

seams.trust_assumption: The IMDSv1 metadata endpoint trusts mere network reachability to the link-local address as authorization to mint role credentials, requiring no session token, so any process or proxied request that can reach 169.254.169.254 is treated as the instance itself.
seams.violation: An attacker leverages a request-forwarding primitive or on-box foothold to GET the IMDSv1 credentials path with no token challenge, harvesting the instance role's temporary keys — a token-less, network-only trust that IMDSv2's PUT-token hop was introduced to revoke.

techniques.name: IMDSv1 token-less credential retrieval (no PUT-token session required)

seams.trust_assumption: Cluster control-plane and node components trust that network reachability to their ports equals authorization, so an exposed kubelet read/write API, an anonymous-auth API server, or an unauthenticated etcd treats any client that can open the socket as a fully entitled caller.
seams.violation: An attacker reachable on the cluster network drives the kubelet exec/run endpoints, calls an anonymous API server, or reads/writes etcd directly, gaining pod execution and secret access because the component conflated being-reachable with being-authorized.

techniques.name: Unauthenticated kubelet API exec/run on port 10250
Anonymous-auth / unauthenticated API server access
Direct etcd read/write of cluster state and secrets

seams.trust_assumption: The node trusts that a pod admitted with hostPID, hostNetwork, hostPath, or privileged-securityContext was deliberately granted shared access to host namespaces and devices, so a workload running in those inherited contexts is treated as entitled to the host's processes, mounts, and kernel interfaces.
seams.violation: An attacker who lands in a pod scheduled with shared host namespaces or a hostPath mount inherits the node's PID namespace, root filesystem, or device nodes and operates on the host directly — escalating through legitimately-granted context inheritance rather than a kernel escape bug.

techniques.name: hostPID / hostNetwork process and socket access from pod
hostPath mount of node root filesystem or docker socket
Privileged securityContext device and capability abuse

seams.trust_assumption: The API server trusts a presented service-account JWT as the identity of whoever holds it, and the kubelet auto-mounts that token into every pod by default, so a token readable from a compromised container authenticates the attacker as the service account regardless of which workload actually read it.
seams.violation: An attacker reads the auto-mounted /var/run/secrets token (or a broadly-scoped projected token) from a foothold pod and replays it against the API server, assuming the service account's RBAC — a bearer credential whose mere possession the operator could not have prevented from being copied.

techniques.name: Auto-mounted service-account token theft and replay
Over-scoped projected/bound token reuse for cloud workload-identity federation

seams.trust_assumption: The cluster trusts that an image pulled from a registry by tag is the genuine, vetted artifact that name implies, so an unsigned image, a mutable tag, or a typosquatted/public registry reference is run as if its provenance had been verified.
seams.violation: An attacker publishes or substitutes a malicious image under a trusted-looking name or repoints a mutable tag, and the cluster schedules and executes it because tag-as-identity stood in for cryptographic provenance the admission path never checked.

techniques.name: Malicious / typosquatted public-registry image pull
Mutable-tag substitution and missing image-signature (cosign/notary) verification

seams.trust_assumption: The cluster trusts that secret material placed into environment variables, ConfigMaps, or base64-only Kubernetes Secret objects is shielded by namespace and RBAC boundaries, so credentials surfaced into a workload's process environment or an unencrypted etcd object are treated as confined to their intended consumer.
seams.violation: An attacker with read on a pod, ConfigMap, or the Secret API dumps the process environment, reads plaintext ConfigMaps, or decodes base64 Secrets (unencrypted at rest in etcd), harvesting database, registry, and cloud credentials that namespace trust assumed were private.

techniques.name: Secrets-in-environment-variable and /proc/environ harvest
ConfigMap plaintext and base64 Secret decode (no etcd encryption-at-rest)

seams.trust_assumption: The admission layer trusts that the manifest it evaluates is the manifest that will run and that its policy set covers every entry path, so a request shaped to dodge a label selector, hit a failure-open webhook, or mutate after evaluation is admitted as if it had satisfied OPA/Gatekeeper/Kyverno rules.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits failure-open webhook timeouts, namespace/label exemptions, or mutate-after-validate ordering to land a privileged or non-compliant workload, defeating policy whose verdict was trusted as binding over the actual runtime spec.

techniques.name: Validating-webhook failure-open / timeout bypass
Policy exemption / selector gap and mutate-after-validate ordering abuse

seams.trust_assumption: The web proxy and reputation engine trust that a domain's registration age, established WHOIS history, and assigned content-category reflect an organic legitimate purpose, so an aged or categorized domain is allow-listed as benign without re-inspecting who actually controls it now.
seams.violation: The operator pre-ages, buys expired-but-reputable, or pre-submits domains for benign categorization (banking/health) so that when they go live as C2, the proxy passes them on a reputation signal the operator manufactured rather than earned.

techniques.name: Domain aging / expired-domain repurposing for proxy-category trust
Pre-submitted content-categorization abuse (category-as-benignity)

seams.trust_assumption: The inspector trusts that a CA-issued, valid TLS certificate signals an accountable, monitorable identity and that Certificate Transparency logs give defenders early visibility into an operator's domains, so a freely-issued cert on a plausible hostname is read as legitimacy and CT is assumed to surface new infrastructure.
seams.violation: The operator obtains automated free certificates on benign-looking hostnames and exploits CT blind spots — wildcard certs, pre-cert-only logging, and ad-hoc internal CAs — so cert validity launders the host as trustworthy while CT monitoring never sees the leaf actually fronting C2.

techniques.name: Free-CA cert issuance for benign-appearing C2 hostnames
Certificate-Transparency evasion via wildcard / unlogged-leaf certificates

seams.trust_assumption: Fraud, geo, and IP-reputation engines trust that the source IP's network type and location stand in for the actor's identity and intent — a residential/mobile ASN from the expected country reads as a real local user, while datacenter and Tor ranges read as suspicious.
seams.violation: The operator routes traffic through residential-proxy and mobile-LTE egress pools so requests inherit the clean reputation and plausible geolocation of real subscribers, laundering automated or out-of-region activity as a trusted local human and defeating ASN/geo risk scoring.

techniques.name: Residential / mobile proxy egress for geo and IP-reputation laundering
Egress rotation to evade rate-limit and source-reputation blocking

seams.trust_assumption: Platforms and human targets trust that an account's age, accumulated post/connection history, and established activity pattern are evidence of an authentic person, so a seasoned profile with a credible backstory is treated as more legitimate and lower-risk than a fresh registration.
seams.violation: The operator builds and ages sock-puppet accounts — warming them with organic-looking activity and connections over months — so that when used for phishing, social engineering, or platform abuse they pass the account-history legitimacy heuristic the defender relies on instead of identity proof.

techniques.name: Aged burner / sock-puppet persona cultivation for platform trust
Established-persona social-engineering and connection-graph priming

seams.trust_assumption: A computer-use or browser-use agent treats the screenshot or accessibility tree it captures as a faithful rendering of what the human operator would see, so the pixels and DOM labels it perceives are accepted as ground truth about page state before it clicks.
seams.violation: An attacker controls regions the agent's vision model attends to but the human never visually parses the same way (off-viewport DOM nodes, CSS-hidden but accessibility-exposed text, near-invisible overlays, adversarial pixel patches on a 'Confirm' button) so the agent acts on a screen state that does not match the real consequence of the action.

techniques.name: DOM/accessibility-tree label injection that diverges from human-visible rendering
Adversarial UI patch steering vision-grounded click coordinates

seams.rationale: Computer-use agents (Claude/OpenAI operator-style, browser-use frameworks) ground actions in a screenshot plus an accessibility/DOM snapshot, but the agent's perception of the page and a human's perception are produced by entirely different pipelines, so a page can be crafted to read one way to a vision model and another to a person. Existing web-security tooling checks server-side content and existing prompt-injection filters scan text strings, but neither models the gap between rendered pixels, the accessibility tree the agent ingests, and the action the agent ultimately commits; clickjacking defenses (framebusting, X-Frame-Options) assume a human victim's eyes, not a model's attention map. There is essentially no tooling that diffs 'what the agent perceived' against 'what executing the click actually does,' and no detection that flags a perception/action mismatch.
seams.suggested_research: Build a harness that renders adversarial pages, captures the agent's screenshot+accessibility-tree, records the chosen action, and measures divergence between human-visible affordance and committed action; quantify how many leading computer-use agents can be steered to click a destructive control hidden from the human render.

seams.trust_assumption: A multimodal agent pipeline assumes its text-based safety/injection filter has seen everything semantically meaningful in an artifact, so once the visible text passes, the image, audio, or PDF object is treated as inert payload safe to hand to the model.
seams.violation: Instructions are carried in channels the text filter never reads but the model does decode (EXIF/XMP fields, PDF annotation and form-field streams, ID3 tags, alt-text, steganographic or sub-audible audio, glyph-substituted text inside images) so a prompt-injection passes the guardrail and reaches the model out-of-band.

techniques.name: Instruction smuggling via image/audio/document metadata fields
Cross-modal injection bypassing a text-only safety classifier

seams.rationale: Production agent stacks bolt a text-classifier guardrail in front of the model, but multimodal models ingest the raw bytes of images, audio, and PDFs and recover instructions from metadata and non-rendered streams that the guardrail's OCR/transcription step often skips. The defensive assumption is single-modality: the filter and the model do not share a view of the artifact, so the trust boundary is drawn around the wrong surface. Current scanners strip macros and known-bad signatures from files but do not normalize across the exact decoding path a given multimodal model uses, and no detector flags the case where the model acted on content that never appeared in the filtered text stream.
seams.suggested_research: Map, per frontier multimodal model, which metadata/stream fields survive into the token stream; build a differential fuzzer that injects identical instructions across modalities and metadata fields and reports which bypass the deployed text guardrail.

seams.trust_assumption: An agent runtime that caches tool/function-call results for cost and latency assumes a cached result is a faithful stand-in for re-executing the tool, so a cache hit is trusted exactly as a fresh, authenticated tool call would be.
seams.violation: An attacker who can influence a tool's output once (a transient poisoned web fetch, a single malicious MCP response, a manipulated cache key collision) gets that result frozen in the cache and re-served as authoritative across later runs, sessions, or other users, long after the upstream source is clean.

techniques.name: Poison-once / serve-many agent tool-result cache injection
Cache-key collision to cross-contaminate tool results

seams.rationale: To cut token and API costs, agent frameworks (and prompt-caching layers) increasingly memoize tool outputs keyed on call arguments, but this turns a one-time output-influence into persistent control: the upstream source can be fixed and the agent still consumes the poisoned snapshot. This differs from vector-store or memory persistence because the carrier is the ephemeral-by-assumption tool-result cache, which has no provenance or freshness semantics and is rarely scoped per-tenant. No current tooling treats tool-result caches as a trust surface, there is no integrity/TTL discipline tied to source freshness, and SOC telemetry does not record cache hits as a distinct event, so a poisoned result is invisible after the originating fetch scrolls out of logs.
seams.suggested_research: Audit popular agent frameworks for tool-result cache scope, key derivation, and TTL; demonstrate a poison-once-serve-many attack and prototype a provenance-stamped cache that re-validates against source freshness before a hit is trusted.

seams.trust_assumption: A human-in-the-loop confirmation gate assumes each approval prompt represents a fresh, independent act of human judgment, so the design trusts that the operator reads and reasons about every action the agent surfaces for sign-off.
seams.violation: The agent's own high-frequency benign confirmations condition the operator into reflexive rubber-stamping, so an attacker who can inject one malicious action into the stream gets it approved by a human whose 'consent' has been behaviorally trained into a reflex by the legitimate workflow itself.

techniques.name: Confirmation-stream conditioning to harvest reflexive approval
Malicious action camouflaged in a high-volume approval queue

seams.rationale: Unlike MFA push fatigue, the conditioning here is endogenous: the legitimate agent workflow generates so many trivially-safe approval prompts that the human's vigilance decays as a direct function of the product's own design, making the eventual malicious approval a feature of normal operation rather than an external bombing campaign. Vendors treat HITL as a sufficient safety control and instrument approval rate as a success metric, not as an attack surface; there is no tooling that models approval-prompt salience or measures operator habituation, and no detection that distinguishes a deliberated approval from a conditioned reflex. The seam is the assumption that a click equals judgment.
seams.suggested_research: Run a study measuring approval latency and error rate as a function of prior benign-prompt volume; design and test 'salience budgeting' (rate-limiting/escalating friction for consequential actions) and detect anomalous fast-approval patterns as a habituation signal.

seams.trust_assumption: Operators trust that a model or guardrail which passes its published safety eval / red-team benchmark will refuse the real-world class of behavior the eval was meant to represent, treating the eval score as a proxy for the underlying intent it is supposed to measure.
seams.violation: An attacker crafts inputs that fall in the gap between the finite eval distribution and the true intent, exploiting that vendors optimize against the published benchmark (Goodhart): the attack satisfies the model's learned 'pass the eval' shortcut while violating the spirit the eval claims to certify.

techniques.name: Guardrail bypass via distribution gap outside the published eval set
Specification-gaming exploitation of a benchmark-overfit safety filter

seams.rationale: The 2025-2027 safety-eval ecosystem rewards visible benchmark scores, so models and guardrails are tuned until they pass widely-published evals; because those evals are finite and public, defenders unwittingly train to the test, and the certified-safe artifact is brittle precisely on the inputs the benchmark did not enumerate. This is a provenance seam: the eval result is trusted as evidence about a behavior class it cannot actually guarantee. Standard practice has no tooling to measure the gap between eval coverage and intended behavior, and no detection fires when an input lands in the uncovered region, because by construction it looks like exactly the safe behavior the model demonstrated on the benchmark.
seams.suggested_research: Build a coverage-vs-intent differential: hold out adversarial paraphrases semantically equivalent to eval items but outside their surface distribution, and measure refusal-rate collapse across released models/guardrails to quantify per-eval overfit.

seams.trust_assumption: A deployment that loads a downloaded fine-tune or LoRA/adapter on top of a vetted base model assumes the adapter is a benign behavioral delta, so the composed model inherits the trust placed in the base weights and is run with the base model's safety expectations.
seams.violation: A small, cheaply-distributed adapter encodes a backdoor, alignment-stripping, or trigger-conditioned behavior that activates only on attacker-chosen inputs, so the composition silently overrides the base model's safety while presenting as the trusted base lineage.

techniques.name: Backdoored / alignment-stripping LoRA adapter inheriting base-model trust
Trigger-conditioned adapter behavior evading pre-deployment eval

seams.rationale: Adapters are tiny, abundant, and shared on open hubs with far weaker provenance hygiene than base models, yet they can fully rewrite a model's safety behavior at low cost; deployers reason about the base model's reputation and forget that the runtime artifact is base+adapter. Unlike a poisoned dependency in code, there is no source to diff, signatures rarely cover adapter weights, and the malicious behavior can be made conditional so it never appears in pre-deployment testing. Current model-supply-chain tooling focuses on full-checkpoint hashing and license scanning; there is no practical adapter-level integrity attestation or behavioral-delta auditing, and no detection that the loaded composition differs in safety from the base lineage it claims.
seams.suggested_research: Develop adapter behavioral-delta fingerprinting (compare base vs base+adapter refusal/behavior on a probe battery) and a signed adapter-provenance scheme; survey adapters on public hubs for trigger-conditioned alignment regressions.

seams.trust_assumption: An OAuth 2.1 authorization server that supports dynamic client registration for MCP/agent clients trusts that a client which can self-register and complete a consent flow is a legitimate application the user intended to authorize, treating successful registration plus user consent as adequate provenance for an OAuth client identity.
seams.violation: An attacker stands up a rogue MCP server or agent client that self-registers, advertises a benign name/logo, and rides the user's habituated consent or a manipulated agent into granting it scoped tokens, so an unvetted ephemeral client obtains durable delegated authority with no human-recognizable app identity behind it.

techniques.name: Rogue self-registered OAuth client via dynamic client registration
Consent-phishing through agent-mediated authorization flow

seams.rationale: The MCP authorization spec leans on OAuth 2.1 dynamic client registration so agents can connect to arbitrary servers without pre-provisioning, which removes the human vetting step that traditional OAuth app onboarding relied on; the authorization server now grants tokens to clients with no established identity or reputation. The risk is amplified when the consenting party is an agent or a habituated user clicking through an agent-initiated flow. Enterprise OAuth governance tooling (app consent dashboards, publisher verification) assumes a finite set of pre-registered apps and does not model a stream of ephemeral self-registered MCP clients; there is no detection for anomalous DCR volume or for tokens held by clients that lack a verifiable publisher.
seams.suggested_research: Inventory how major IdPs/AS implementations handle MCP DCR (rate limits, publisher attestation, consent UX); demonstrate a rogue-MCP token-grab and prototype DCR reputation/attestation gating plus anomalous-registration detection.

seams.trust_assumption: A multi-tenant LLM serving layer that shares prefix/prompt caches across requests for efficiency assumes cache hits are a private latency optimization, so it treats time-to-first-token as carrying no confidential information about other tenants' or sessions' prompt contents.
seams.violation: An attacker measures response latency to detect whether a chosen prefix is already cached, inferring that another tenant or an earlier privileged session contained that exact prefix, and recovers secret prompt content (system prompts, document fragments, PII) token-by-token through a shared-cache timing side channel.

techniques.name: Shared prompt-prefix cache timing oracle for cross-request content inference
Cache-hit confirmation of secret system-prompt or document tokens

seams.rationale: Prefix/prompt caching is now a default cost optimization in major inference stacks, and when the cache is keyed on content and shared across tenants or sessions, the resulting timing differential is a classic side channel reachable purely through the public API with no host access. Defenders treat caching as a performance feature governed by a billing/latency SLA, not as a confidentiality boundary, so the trust assumption that latency is information-free goes unexamined. There is no tooling to test an endpoint for content-keyed shared caching, no per-tenant cache-isolation guarantee in most offerings, and detection is absent because a timing-probe request is indistinguishable from ordinary traffic at the application layer.
seams.suggested_research: Probe production inference APIs for cross-tenant prefix-cache timing signals; quantify token-recovery bandwidth and evaluate mitigations (per-tenant cache partitioning, constant-time cache-miss padding, cache scoping policies).

seams.trust_assumption: A code-interpreter / sandbox tool assumes its job is to contain code execution (filesystem, CPU, process isolation), so any network egress the sandbox is permitted is treated as a benign convenience rather than a confidentiality boundary the model's untrusted-generated code can weaponize.
seams.violation: Prompt-injected or attacker-influenced content drives the agent to emit code that runs in the sandbox and uses the permitted egress (package installs, API fetches, DNS) to exfiltrate the agent's context, secrets, or fetched documents, turning execution containment that ignores egress into a data-out channel.

techniques.name: Data exfiltration via sandbox egress in model-generated code
DNS / package-index covert channel from code interpreter

seams.rationale: Code-interpreter sandboxes are engineered around execution isolation, and many allow outbound network access for pip/npm installs or legitimate API calls; the threat model is 'don't let code escape the box,' not 'the box itself is driven by untrusted model output that can mail data out.' Because the code is generated under possible injection influence, the sandbox is a confused deputy with a network card. Classic DLP and egress monitoring are not wired into ephemeral per-request sandboxes, allowlists are coarse (whole package registries, broad CDNs), and there is no provenance link between what entered the agent's context and what the sandbox subsequently transmits, so exfiltration looks like a normal dependency fetch.
seams.suggested_research: Survey hosted code-interpreter offerings for default egress posture; demonstrate context-exfiltration via package-index and DNS channels and prototype egress allowlisting tied to taint-tracking of sensitive context entering the sandbox.

seams.trust_assumption: An agent deployment treats its system prompt, tool schemas, and guardrail instructions as a confidential control layer the user cannot read, so its injection defenses are designed assuming the attacker is operating blind to the exact wording and tool surface they must defeat.
seams.violation: The attacker first extracts the hidden system prompt and tool definitions (via leakage prompts, refusal-boundary probing, error messages, or token-level reconstruction), then crafts an injection that precisely targets the disclosed instruction wording and tool names, collapsing the security-by-obscurity gap the defenses silently depended on.

techniques.name: System-prompt and tool-schema extraction enabling tailored injection
Refusal-boundary probing to reconstruct hidden guardrail wording

seams.rationale: Most deployed agents implicitly rely on the secrecy of their system prompt and tool inventory for injection resistance, but extraction is reliably achievable and the leaked specifics (exact instruction phrasing, tool names, argument schemas) turn a generic injection into a surgical one that names the very rules and tools it must subvert. The seam is a provenance failure: defenders trust that the control layer is unobservable. Tooling exists to attempt extraction but not to measure how much a given deployment's defense degrades once its prompt is known, and there is no detection that links an earlier extraction-style interaction to a later precisely-targeted injection from the same actor.
seams.suggested_research: Measure injection success rate with vs without prior system-prompt knowledge across agent products to quantify the obscurity dependency; build a detector that correlates extraction-probing sessions with subsequent tailored injection attempts.

seams.trust_assumption: When a parent agent spawns a sub-agent and passes along its task context, it assumes that context is sanitized internal state, so the sub-agent treats inherited instructions and data as trusted parent tasking rather than as content that may have been shaped by upstream untrusted input.
seams.violation: Untrusted content the parent absorbed (a poisoned document, web result, or prior injection) rides into the spawned sub-agent's context unfiltered, and the sub-agent — often holding different or broader tools — executes attacker intent as if it were authentic parent direction, with no boundary re-validation at the spawn.

techniques.name: Unsanitized context inheritance across agent spawn boundary
Privilege/tool-scope escalation via sub-agent with broader capabilities

seams.rationale: Orchestration frameworks (planner/worker, supervisor/sub-agent, fan-out research patterns) pass context across spawn boundaries as if it were trusted internal handoff, but that context routinely contains untrusted material the parent ingested; the spawn is a trust-inheritance point with no re-sanitization, and the sub-agent may have a different tool scope, so injection can effectively escalate by jumping to a more capable agent. This is distinct from inter-agent trust-token forgery (peer authentication) — the failure here is taint propagation through context inheritance within a single trust domain. No framework taint-tracks content across spawns, and detection is absent because the sub-agent's actions look like legitimate delegated work.
seams.suggested_research: Instrument popular multi-agent frameworks to taint-track untrusted content across spawn boundaries; demonstrate a parent->sub-agent escalation where the sub-agent's broader tools execute injected intent, and prototype context-sanitization/scope-narrowing at spawn.

seams.trust_assumption: A RAG or agentic-search system presents citations as verifiable provenance, so the human consumer trusts that a cited source link substantiates the accompanying claim and that the agent quoted or grounded the claim in that real source.
seams.violation: The agent emits a fabricated or mismatched citation — a plausible but nonexistent source, a real URL that does not actually support the claim, or an attacker-planted document crafted to look authoritative — and the human accepts the unverified claim precisely because it appears cited, laundering false or attacker-chosen content through the trust signal of provenance.

techniques.name: Fabricated or mismatched citation laundering an unsupported claim
Attacker-planted authoritative-looking source for citation hijack

seams.rationale: Citations are sold as the answer to LLM hallucination, so users and downstream automations lower their scrutiny on cited claims, yet the citation is generated by the same untrusted-text-conditioned model and is frequently not actually checked against the linked source; this converts the provenance signal into an attack surface where a wrong or attacker-supplied citation makes false content more trusted, not less. The defensive assumption is that 'cited' implies 'grounded.' Tooling that auto-verifies whether a cited source genuinely entails the claim is nascent at best, and there is no detection for the human-facing failure mode where a confidently-cited but unsupported claim drives a decision; SOC and content-integrity tooling do not inspect citation-claim entailment.
seams.suggested_research: Build a citation-entailment verifier that fetches each cited source and scores whether it supports the claim, measure fabrication/mismatch rates across deployed RAG products, and test whether displaying entailment scores changes human over-trust on cited output.

seams.trust_assumption: An auto-remediation/SOAR playbook treats alert fields it ingests (hostname, username, file path, URL, process command line, IOC) as already-sanitized parameters, safe to interpolate directly into the API calls, shell actions, and enrichment queries it fires without an analyst in the loop.
seams.violation: An attacker who controls a value that lands in an alert field (a username, a filename, a User-Agent, a DNS label) plants payload that the playbook expands into the remediation action itself, turning the defender's response automation into attacker-directed command/API execution against the very assets it was meant to protect.

techniques.name: Alert-field to playbook-action injection (host isolation, account disable, firewall rule) via attacker-controlled telemetry
Enrichment-query injection turning SOAR API tokens into attacker-scoped calls

seams.rationale: SOC teams adopted no-touch SOAR/auto-remediation faster than they hardened the data path into it: the playbook author assumes alert fields are trustworthy because they came from the EDR/SIEM, but those fields are populated from attacker-influenced artifacts (process names, file paths, web logs). The defender's own privileged automation runner now executes against parameters the adversary chose, and because the action is a 'response' it is allowlisted, unlogged as suspicious, and runs with broad cross-platform tokens. There is no public tooling that fuzzes the alert-field-to-action expansion and almost no detection that treats a remediation action as a potential injection sink.
seams.suggested_research: Build a harness that seeds malicious values into observable artifact types (filename, username, UA, DNS) and traces which fields reach SOAR action templates unescaped in Splunk SOAR/Sentinel/Tines/Cortex XSOAR; propose taint-tracking from telemetry ingestion to playbook action sinks.

seams.trust_assumption: A SOC's detection-as-code pipeline trusts that a Sigma/YARA/Sentinel rule merged from the shared community or vendor rule repository, having passed CI lint and review, faithfully covers the TTP its title and metadata claim to detect.
seams.violation: An attacker who lands a subtly over-scoped filter, a broken field mapping, or a logic-narrowing exception into the shared rule corpus ships a detection that looks present and green in coverage dashboards but silently excludes the exact behavior the adversary intends to run, blinding every downstream SOC that syncs the repo.

techniques.name: Poisoned Sigma/detection rule with coverage-preserving exclusion clause via PR to shared repo
Field-mapping/log-source desync that renders a rule non-firing while reporting as enabled

seams.rationale: Detection-as-code moved the SOC from hand-tuned rules to git-synced community rule repos (Sigma HQ, Elastic detection-rules, Sentinel content hub) reviewed by humans who check syntax and intent prose, not adversarial coverage. A reviewer cannot easily tell that an added `filter` or a renamed field reduces true-positive coverage to zero while the rule still parses, deploys, and shows green in MITRE-coverage heatmaps. This is a provenance seam: the SOC trusts the rule for where it came from, not for what it actually matches against real events. No tooling differentially tests a rule's firing surface before/after a PR, and the blind spot persists unnoticed until the excluded TTP is used.
seams.suggested_research: Build a regression harness that replays a labeled event corpus against rule PRs and flags coverage deltas; mine Sigma/Elastic repo history for exclusion clauses correlated with known TTPs and measure mean time-to-detect a deliberately weakening PR.

seams.trust_assumption: A cloud-delivered EDR/UEBA detection model trusts that the streamed endpoint telemetry it continuously trains its behavioral baseline on reflects honest, representative normal activity for that host or identity, so a behavior that fits the learned baseline is benign by construction.
seams.violation: An attacker who has low-and-slow presence drip-feeds the malicious behavior into the telemetry stream at a rate below the model's anomaly threshold, shifting the rolling baseline until the eventual full-tempo action sits inside the now-normalized envelope and never scores as anomalous.

techniques.name: Slow-drip behavioral baseline shifting against cloud UEBA/EDR ML scoring
Adversarial concept-drift induction to widen the learned normal envelope

seams.rationale: Vendors moved detection from static signatures to continuously-retraining cloud behavioral baselines (UEBA, EDR ML) far faster than they built integrity controls on the training stream. The check-to-use gap is the model's whole adaptation window: behavior that is anomalous on day one becomes baseline on day thirty if introduced gradually, and the defender has no notion that the baseline was steered rather than learned. Unlike classic evasion (hiding from a fixed rule), this attacks the model's plasticity. There is no public tooling to measure how much an adversary can move a given vendor's baseline per unit time, and no detection distinguishes organic drift from deliberate drift.
seams.suggested_research: Empirically characterize the per-feature drift rate that stays under-threshold for leading UEBA products; design a poisoning-rate estimator and propose drift-provenance anchoring (immutable early-baseline reference) as a control.

seams.trust_assumption: A relying party trusts a synced FIDO2/passkey assertion as proof of a phishing-resistant hardware-bound authenticator, even though the private key is replicated by the platform vendor's cloud (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, vendor passkey sync) across every device in the user's account and recoverable through that account.
seams.violation: An attacker who compromises the platform account or its recovery path (or downgrades a passkey-enrolled account back to weaker fallback factors) obtains or re-provisions the synced credential off-device, defeating the RP's assumption of device possession without ever touching the user's hardware.

techniques.name: Passkey sync-fabric account takeover to export/replicate the synced credential
Account-recovery downgrade to fallback factor that bypasses the enrolled passkey

seams.rationale: The industry pushed passkeys hard as phishing-resistant and 'hardware-bound', but the consumer reality is synced passkeys whose security collapses to the security of the platform cloud account and, worse, to whatever recovery/fallback factor the RP left enabled for account-recovery UX. Defenders treat a passkey assertion's AAGUID/UV flag as proof of a strong authenticator and rarely inspect whether the credential is sync-eligible or whether a weaker recovery path still exists. The provenance seam: the RP checks the assertion's claimed strength, not where the key actually lives. Tooling to enumerate sync-eligibility and recovery-downgrade paths per RP is nascent and detection of off-device credential re-provisioning is absent.
seams.suggested_research: Map major RPs' acceptance of synced vs device-bound credentials and their residual recovery factors; build a tool that flags RPs accepting sync-eligible passkeys while still permitting SMS/email recovery, quantifying the downgrade surface.

seams.trust_assumption: A conditional-access / zero-trust IdP grants or relaxes access based on a device-compliance and posture signal (managed, patched, EDR-healthy, disk-encrypted) that it treats as a trustworthy attestation, when much of that posture is computed and asserted by a client-side agent the user's device fully controls.
seams.violation: An attacker on the device tampers with the posture agent's reported state or replays a known-good attestation, presenting a non-compliant or compromised endpoint as healthy so the IdP applies the trusted-device policy branch and waives step-up controls.

techniques.name: Client-asserted device-compliance signal forgery against conditional access
Posture-attestation replay / hooking of the compliance agent

seams.rationale: Zero-trust adoption made device posture a primary access gate, but the posture is frequently client-asserted (an agent reports 'compliant') rather than hardware-rooted, and even where a TPM is involved the binding between the attested boot state and the live runtime posture is weak. The IdP authorizes the asserted position, not verified ground truth. Defenders display posture as authoritative in dashboards and gate sensitive apps on it, yet there is little public tooling that systematically forges or replays each vendor's compliance signal, and almost no detection that cross-checks asserted posture against independent server-side observation. Defense automated the gate faster than it rooted the signal.
seams.suggested_research: Inventory which posture attributes per major MDM/IdP (Intune, Jamf, Okta Device Trust) are client-asserted vs hardware-attested; build a posture-spoofing test agent and propose server-corroborated posture as a control.

seams.trust_assumption: An artifact verifier (admission controller, deploy gate, package consumer) trusts a valid SLSA/in-toto provenance attestation as proof that the artifact was built as described, treating a cryptographically verified, correctly-signed attestation as covering the entire build that produced what is being deployed.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits the gap between what the attestation actually binds and what mattered: a pre-build step, a vendored dependency, a post-build mutation, or a subject-digest scope the predicate never covered, so a genuinely signed and verifiable attestation vouches for an artifact whose malicious portion was outside its claim.

techniques.name: Provenance scope gap (attested build step != exploited step) passing verification
Subject/predicate digest-binding mismatch letting an unattested artifact ride a valid attestation

seams.rationale: The ecosystem rushed to 'verify provenance' (Sigstore, SLSA, in-toto, GitHub artifact attestations) and admission controllers now gate on a green signature check. But verification proves the attestation is authentic and well-formed, not that its scope covers the step the attacker abused: dependency resolution, base-image selection, a hermeticity gap, or a digest that binds the wrong subject. Defenders read 'provenance verified' as 'artifact trustworthy', a provenance seam where the receiver checks the label, not the thing. Public tooling to audit the coverage gap between an attestation's predicate and the real build DAG is nascent, and nothing detects a deploy that passed verification while the exploited step lay outside the claim.
seams.suggested_research: Survey real SLSA/in-toto predicates in the wild for which build steps they actually bind; build a scope-coverage linter that diffs the attested DAG against the full build and flags unattested influence on the final digest.

seams.trust_assumption: A relying party releasing secrets to a confidential-computing workload trusts a TEE remote-attestation quote as proof that the enclave is genuine and running unmodified, trusted code on a patched TCB, treating a quote that verifies against the vendor's roots as current and complete.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits the gap between when the TCB was measured/attested and when secrets are used, or relies on a TCB still vulnerable to a known issue that triggered a security-version (TCB recovery) update the verifier failed to enforce, replaying a stale-but-valid quote to a verifier that does not check freshness or minimum SVN.

techniques.name: Stale TEE attestation replay against a verifier missing freshness/nonce binding
TCB-recovery / minimum-SVN downgrade accepted by lax appraisal policy

seams.rationale: Confidential computing (SGX/TDX/SEV-SNP, Azure/GCP confidential VMs, confidential AI inference) is being adopted to release secrets and data to enclaves, and verifiers commonly accept a quote that chains to the vendor root without enforcing nonce freshness or a minimum security-version after a TCB recovery event. The check-to-use gap is real: a quote proves a measurement at issuance, not the live state at secret use, and TCB-recovery enforcement is an appraisal-policy choice operators routinely get wrong. Defenders treat 'attestation passed' as binary trust. Tooling that audits a verifier's freshness and SVN-minimum policy is nascent, and there is no detection for a relying party releasing keys against a stale or under-versioned quote.
seams.suggested_research: Audit common attestation-verification libraries and managed services for default nonce-freshness and minimum-SVN enforcement; build a test that replays an old valid quote and a below-recovery SVN to measure which verifiers accept them.

seams.trust_assumption: An eBPF-based security sensor trusts the kernel event stream it observes (and the order/scope of the hook points it attaches) as a complete, tamper-evident view of system activity, and the kernel trusts a privileged, verifier-passing eBPF program loaded by a capable principal as benign instrumentation.
seams.violation: An attacker who can load eBPF (CAP_BPF/privileged container) or who operates in the gaps between the sensor's hook points uses eBPF itself to filter, mask, or rewrite the very events the defensive eBPF tool relies on, or attaches before/below it, so the security sensor inherits a view its own substrate silently edited.

techniques.name: eBPF-as-rootkit: hook-ordering and map tampering to hide processes/connections from eBPF EDR
Coverage blind spot between sensor hook points (syscall variants, uprobes, ringbuf gaps)

seams.rationale: eBPF became the default substrate for modern runtime security (Falco, Cilium Tetragon, vendor eBPF EDR) much faster than the question 'who watches the eBPF layer' was answered. The defensive tool inherits the trustworthiness of a kernel surface that another privileged eBPF program (or a capable container) can manipulate, and the sensor's coverage is only as complete as its chosen hook set, with documented gaps. This is a context-inheritance seam: the sensor inherits the kernel's view without re-validating that nothing edited it. Offensive eBPF rootkit research exists but mature tooling targeting specific eBPF EDRs is nascent, and there is essentially no detection of eBPF-on-eBPF tampering or hook-ordering attacks.
seams.suggested_research: Enumerate the hook points each major eBPF security tool attaches and map the syscall/event gaps; prototype an eBPF program that masks events from a co-resident eBPF sensor and propose attach-time integrity/ordering attestation.

seams.trust_assumption: A cloud secrets broker / workload-identity-federation trust trusts that an OIDC token from a configured external issuer, whose subject/audience claims match the trust policy, was minted for the specific workload the policy intends, treating a claim-matching token as a faithful binding to that exact non-human principal.
seams.violation: An attacker obtains or causes issuance of a token from the same trusted issuer whose claims satisfy an over-broad or wildcarded subject/audience condition (a different repo, branch, namespace, or service account under the same issuer), assuming the federated identity and exchanging it for cloud credentials the policy never meant to grant.

techniques.name: Workload identity federation subject/audience over-scope (wildcard repo/branch/namespace) credential exchange
Trusted-issuer token misbinding across workloads sharing an OIDC provider

seams.rationale: To kill long-lived secrets, teams adopted keyless workload identity federation (GitHub OIDC to AWS/GCP/Azure, GKE/EKS workload identity, SPIFFE) at scale, but the security collapses to the precision of the trust-policy claim conditions, which are routinely wildcarded for convenience (any branch, any repo in an org, any pod in a namespace). The broker authorizes the position the claim names, not the intent, so a token from a less-trusted sibling workload under the same issuer is accepted. Defenders see 'no static keys' as the win and rarely audit subject-condition tightness. Tooling that enumerates over-broad federation bindings is nascent, and detection of a token-claim-vs-intended-workload mismatch at exchange time is essentially absent.
seams.suggested_research: Build a scanner that parses IAM/role trust policies and federation configs to flag wildcarded subject/audience conditions and ranks blast radius; correlate with which sibling workloads can actually mint a satisfying token.

seams.trust_assumption: A service in a mesh trusts that because a request arrived over mutual-TLS bearing a verified SPIFFE/workload identity from a sidecar, the L7 content of that request (path, method, headers, propagated JWT, on-behalf-of claims) was authorized for that caller, conflating transport-level peer authentication with application-level authorization.
seams.violation: A compromised or over-privileged in-mesh workload that holds a valid mTLS identity sends requests whose L7 semantics it is not entitled to (privileged paths, spoofed forwarded-identity headers, replayed propagated tokens), and downstream services that gate only on the authenticated peer identity grant access the L7 policy should have denied.

techniques.name: Authenticated-peer to L7 authz confusion (privileged path/method abuse inside the mesh)
Forwarded-identity / propagated-JWT header spoof between meshed services

seams.rationale: Service meshes (Istio, Linkerd, Consul) made mTLS-everywhere cheap, and teams adopted it as the security story, but mTLS proves who the peer is, not what the peer may do at L7. Many meshes default to peer-authn with permissive or absent fine-grained authorization policy, and forwarded-identity headers are trusted inside the trust boundary. A single compromised workload inherits the mesh-wide ability to reach services its application-layer entitlements never granted. This is a context-inheritance seam: downstream inherits the transport's authenticated trust without re-validating L7 intent. Tooling to map mesh authz gaps and forge forwarded-identity is nascent, and detection of intra-mesh L7 authz violations is rare because the traffic is encrypted-and-trusted.
seams.suggested_research: Build a mesh-authz mapper that diffs configured AuthorizationPolicies against the actual service call graph to find peer-authenticated-but-unauthorized edges; test forwarded-identity header trust between sidecars.

seams.trust_assumption: A just-in-time PAM / privileged-access-management system trusts that an elevation request approved through its workflow (justification text, approver click, ticket reference) represents a legitimate, scoped, time-bounded grant, treating the approval event as proof the elevated session will be used as the justification described.
seams.violation: An attacker abuses the elevation workflow itself by self-approving via a controlled or auto-approval path, social-engineering an approver with a plausible justification, racing the activation/deactivation window, or chaining a benign-looking JIT grant into standing privilege, so a procedurally approved elevation becomes attacker-held privilege the system records as legitimate.

techniques.name: JIT elevation self-approval / auto-approval path and approver social engineering
Activation-window race / residual-session abuse after JIT grant expiry

seams.rationale: Organizations replaced standing admin with JIT/PAM elevation (Entra PIM, AWS IAM Identity Center, CyberArk, Teleport) on the premise that approval workflow plus time-boxing equals control. But the workflow trusts the approval event as proof of intent, and the seams are everywhere: auto-approval rules, self-approval in thin teams, approvers rubber-stamping AI-plausible justifications, and sessions/tokens that outlive the JIT window. The system authorizes the approved position, not the actual use, and records the abuse as a legitimate grant, which corrupts the audit trail defenders rely on. There is little tooling that probes a tenant's JIT auto-approval and window-race surface, and detection of post-expiry residual-session abuse is nascent.
seams.suggested_research: Map JIT/PAM products' auto-approval, self-approval, and token-vs-grant lifetime mismatches; build a tester for activation-window races and residual-session persistence after JIT deactivation, and quantify approver susceptibility to LLM-generated justifications.

seams.trust_assumption: An OT control system (HMI/SCADA copilot session) trusts that any setpoint change, valve command, or alarm acknowledgement arriving through the operator console carries the deliberation of a certified human operator who weighed the physical-process consequences before clicking, so it applies no independent safety judgment to a console-originated write.
seams.violation: An LLM operations copilot wired into the HMI translates a prompt-injected or hallucinated 'recommendation' directly into a control write at machine speed, and the control system actuates it as a vetted operator decision because the seam never distinguished the agent's keystrokes from a trained human's, collapsing the human-judgment safety layer the console authority was calibrated for.

techniques.name: Agent-driven HMI setpoint/command write without operator deliberation
Prompt-injected plant-copilot recommendation actuated as operator decision

seams.rationale: Vendors are shipping LLM 'plant copilots' and natural-language HMI assistants into SCADA in 2025-2026 with read/write integration, but every OT safety model assumes the entity at the console is a slow, accountable, judgment-bearing human; the ICS access-control and alarm-management layers have zero notion of an agentic actor that can issue thousands of physically-consequential writes per minute from text input it cannot itself verify. There is no detection that separates agent-originated console writes from human ones and no tooling to rate-limit or require dual-authorization on agent-issued physical-process commands, so this is the highest-value least-tooled seam where AI authority lands on physical actuation.
seams.suggested_research: Build an HMI shim that tags console writes with actor-provenance (human vs agent session) and prototype a dual-authorization gate that forces a separate human confirmation for any agent-originated write crossing a process-safety threshold; demonstrate prompt-injection of a vendor plant-copilot into an unauthorized setpoint change on a simulated SIS-protected loop.

seams.trust_assumption: An OT protocol-translation layer (an agent that converts natural-language intent into Modbus/OPC-UA/DNP3 function codes) is trusted to faithfully carry the human approval context that was attached to the original high-level request all the way down to the wire-level write, so the field device treats the emitted function code as an already-approved engineering action.
seams.violation: The agent decomposes one approved high-level goal into many low-level protocol writes, but the human approval gate was bound to the natural-language intent and never re-checked at the function-code boundary, so injected or over-broad sub-steps reach the controller carrying inherited approval they were never granted, turning a single 'okay' into unbounded direct process control.

techniques.name: Approval-context inheritance across NL-to-fieldbus translation
Sub-step expansion below the human authorization boundary

seams.rationale: MCP-style tool servers that expose OT protocols ('write_register', 'set_coil') to agents are emerging in 2025-2026 industrial-AI pilots, and they bind approval to the human's chat-level request rather than to each generated protocol primitive. Because the translation step is exactly where the human-readable intent stops and the machine-readable command begins, it is the natural place for the approval semantics to evaporate, yet no existing OT IDS or agent guardrail re-validates that each emitted function code is covered by the scope the human actually approved. This diagonal (agent context-inheritance landing on insecure-by-design fieldbus) has no detection and no tooling.
seams.suggested_research: Design an approval-binding protocol that cryptographically scopes a human authorization to a bounded set of resulting fieldbus writes (target addresses, value ranges, count) and rejects any agent-emitted function code outside that envelope; red-team an OPC-UA MCP server by injecting extra writes into an approved batch.

seams.trust_assumption: A fleet-telematics / remote-vehicle-command backend trusts that the remote actuation requests it forwards to vehicles (door unlock, immobilizer toggle, remote-start, geofence command) originate from an authenticated human dispatcher whose request reflected a verified real-world need, and the vehicle gateway trusts a backend-signed command as a legitimate dispatcher action.
seams.violation: An LLM fleet-management agent holding the dispatcher's telematics scope is steered by injected task text (a poisoned support ticket, a malicious route note) into emitting bulk remote-actuation commands, and the telematics backend relays them to vehicles as authentic dispatcher intent, translating manipulated agent reasoning into physical CAN-level actuation across an entire fleet at once.

techniques.name: Agent-issued bulk remote-vehicle command via telematics scope
Injected fleet task expanded to mass remote actuation

seams.rationale: Fleet operators are deploying AI dispatch/ops agents on top of telematics APIs (Geotab, Samsara-class) that expose remote-command primitives, and those APIs were built to trust a human dispatcher behind an OAuth scope. The agent inherits that scope but its 'intent' is derived from untrusted text it ingests (tickets, emails, route data), so a data-to-control injection becomes physical actuation against many vehicles simultaneously, blowing past the one-vehicle-at-a-time human pace the command APIs assumed. No telematics platform today detects agent-originated command bursts or anomalous fan-out, and no automotive IDS sees the cloud-side seam, leaving this AI-to-CAN diagonal entirely untooled.
seams.suggested_research: Prototype a telematics-side anomaly gate that profiles human dispatcher command cadence and blocks agent-driven fan-out (e.g., >N immobilizer/unlock commands per minute) pending human re-auth; demonstrate ticket-borne prompt injection driving a fleet agent to mass remote-unlock in a sandbox.

seams.trust_assumption: A mobile MDM/UEM platform trusts that an administrator-scoped token performs a remote wipe, configuration-profile push, or CA-installation only as a deliberate human admin action against a deliberately-chosen device set, because the platform's authority model and rate limits were sized to a human admin's working pace.
seams.violation: An LLM IT-operations agent holding the MDM admin token is prompt-injected (via a helpdesk message or device-naming string it reads) into pushing a malicious configuration profile, rogue trust CA, or mass remote-wipe across the enrolled estate, and the platform executes it as authentic admin intent because nothing distinguishes the agent's API calls from the human admin's or constrains their blast radius.

techniques.name: Agent-driven mass MDM remote wipe / policy push
Agent-installed rogue MDM trust CA / config profile

seams.rationale: Enterprises are connecting AI IT-ops copilots to Intune/Jamf/Workspace ONE admin APIs in 2025-2026 to automate enrollment and policy chores, but the destructive primitives (wipe, profile push, CA install) were gated only by 'an admin did this' assumptions and human-scale throughput. An agent inherits the full admin scope and can be steered by attacker-controlled text into estate-wide destructive or trust-subverting actions far faster than a human would, and current MDM audit logs cannot tell agent calls from human ones. This is the agent-authority-at-machine-speed primitive landing on mobile fleet management, with detection only nascent and no purpose-built tooling.
seams.suggested_research: Build an MDM API broker that requires step-up human approval for destructive/trust-changing operations above a device-count threshold and tags every call with agent-vs-human provenance; demonstrate helpdesk-ticket injection driving an IT-ops agent to push a rogue trust CA to all enrolled devices.

seams.trust_assumption: An in-vehicle or in-home voice-agent trusts that audio captured by its microphone is an intentional spoken command from an authorized, physically-present human, so it forwards the transcribed text into its LLM-tool loop with the standing authority of the device owner.
seams.violation: An attacker injects inaudible (ultrasonic/dolphin) or RF-replayed audio that the microphone transcribes into commands the human never uttered, and the agent acts on them with owner authority, driving vehicle/home actuation (unlock, start, climate, garage) because the perception boundary treats any decodable audio as an authenticated human utterance.

techniques.name: Ultrasonic/inaudible voice-command injection (DolphinAttack-class) into LLM voice agent
RF/replay audio injection driving agent tool actuation

seams.rationale: Inaudible-voice injection was demonstrated against classic voice assistants years ago, but the 2025-2026 shift is that the voice front-end now feeds an agentic LLM with broad tool/actuation scope (vehicle commands, smart-home, payments), so a perception-layer spoof no longer triggers one canned skill but an open-ended reasoning loop with the owner's authority. The seam is the unverified jump from acoustic signal to trusted human intent, now multiplied by agent capability. No deployed voice-agent performs liveness/anti-ultrasonic detection on the audio channel before granting agent authority, and there is no tooling that binds an utterance to a verified present speaker for agent actions, leaving this RF-perception-to-agent diagonal entirely open.
seams.suggested_research: Investigate ultrasonic-band and replay-artifact detection as a precondition gate before a voice-agent may invoke any physical-actuation tool, plus speaker-liveness binding; demonstrate dolphin-attack injection driving a car voice-agent to unlock/start in a parked test vehicle.

seams.trust_assumption: An agent that consumes RFID/badge/BLE/Wi-Fi telemetry trusts those signals as ground-truth proof of a named human's physical presence or location, so it uses 'person X is at the door / in the building / near the terminal' as an authenticated identity-and-context fact to make access, approval, or automation decisions.
seams.violation: An attacker clones a static badge UID, relays a BLE token, or spoofs Wi-Fi/beacon presence so the agent ingests a forged presence signal as a verified identity claim, and the agent then unlocks doors, approves co-located actions, or escalates context on the strength of a fabricated physical fact it had no way to authenticate.

techniques.name: Cloned/relayed RF credential ingested by agent as presence proof
Spoofed BLE/Wi-Fi presence feeding agent identity-context decision

seams.rationale: RF presence spoofing (badge cloning, BLE relay) is a mature wireless attack, but the new bridge is that 2025-2026 building/automation agents treat presence telemetry as an authenticated identity-and-context input that drives downstream tool actions, fusing a P3 provenance failure with P2 authority. Classic physical access control at least mapped a badge to one door; an agent generalizes a spoofed presence fact into arbitrary co-located reasoning and actuation. No agent framework today carries provenance/assurance metadata on presence signals or refuses to act on unauthenticated RF as ground truth, so this wireless-to-agent identity diagonal is untooled and undetected.
seams.suggested_research: Define a presence-assurance metadata schema (signal type, anti-relay/liveness evidence, confidence) that agents must check before treating RF telemetry as identity, and prototype agent guardrails that downgrade unauthenticated presence to a non-authoritative hint; demonstrate badge-clone-driven agent door-unlock.

seams.trust_assumption: An AI fleet-operations agent that selects and pushes firmware/OTA images is trusted to have established the image's provenance — its own narrated 'I verified the signature and changelog' is accepted as the provenance check — so the IoT/edge fleet treats an agent-initiated update as authorized and authentic.
seams.violation: The agent rationalizes an unsigned, swapped, or downgraded image as legitimate (it 'read' an attacker-supplied changelog or release page and concluded the build was fine) and pushes it to the fleet, and the devices accept it because the human-in-the-loop assumed the agent performed real cryptographic verification that it never actually enforced, persisting attacker firmware at fleet scale.

techniques.name: Agent-narrated firmware verification standing in for real signature check
Agent-pushed downgrade / swapped OTA image across IoT fleet

seams.rationale: AI ops agents are starting to drive OTA rollouts for IoT/edge fleets in 2025-2026, and the dangerous novelty is that an LLM's natural-language assertion of 'verified' is being treated by operators as equivalent to a cryptographic provenance check, when the agent has no reliable way to bind a build to a trusted signer and is itself steerable by the very release notes / repo pages it reads. This converts the classic firmware-provenance gap into an AI-mediated confused-deputy: provenance is asserted by a manipulable narrator rather than enforced by a verifier. No tooling forces agents to surface and gate on actual signature-verification evidence, and no fleet detection flags agent-initiated unsigned/downgrade pushes.
seams.suggested_research: Require agents to emit machine-checkable verification artifacts (signer identity, signature result, anti-rollback version proof) that a separate non-LLM gate validates before any OTA push proceeds; demonstrate a poisoned release page convincing an ops agent to roll out an attacker image fleet-wide.

seams.trust_assumption: A PKI/certificate-issuance pipeline (ACME endpoint, internal CA, cloud private-CA) trusts that a requester holding issuance authority is binding a name to a key only after validating that the requester legitimately controls that identity, and treats an automated agent's issuance/approval calls as authorized administrative intent.
