seams.suggested_research: Build a multi-source time-sanity monitor that bounds GNSS-disciplined time against a disciplined free-running oscillator's predicted drift envelope and flags any slew exceeding physical plausibility before loss-of-lock fires; red-team a PTP grandmaster with an SDR slow-slew profile and measure how far PMU/5G consumers can be walked while the lock LED stays green.

seams.trust_assumption: An autonomous platform's navigation/guidance stack (drone flight controller, ag/mining autonomy, maritime ECDIS autopilot, last-mile robot) trusts the GNSS fix as the absolute ground-truth anchor that corrects its inertial dead-reckoning, so a plausibly-continuous position stream is fused into the state estimator and converted directly into actuation (waypoint, heading, geofence, return-to-home) without the fix ever competing against an independent absolute reference.
seams.violation: An attacker injects a coherent spoofed GNSS constellation that smoothly captures the receiver and then drags the reported position along an attacker-chosen trajectory consistent with vehicle dynamics, so the autonomy treats spoofed data as control input — steering the platform, triggering a false geofence breach, or commanding return-to-home toward attacker coordinates — because the state estimator weights the externally-supplied fix as truth over its own physics.

techniques.name: Coherent capture-and-drag GNSS trajectory injection
Spoofed-fix overriding INS fusion as actuation input

seams.rationale: GNSS position spoofing is demonstrated in the literature, but the frontier seam for 2025-2027 is the data->control coupling inside increasingly autonomous platforms where the fix is not merely displayed to a human but fed directly into a closed-loop estimator that actuates. The trust violated is that an externally-radiated position is ground truth that should dominate the platform's own inertial physics; a dynamics-consistent capture-and-drag defeats the cheap consistency checks (jump detection, signal-strength) most COTS receivers ship. This differs from manual-navigation spoofing because the victim is an automated control loop with machine-speed reaction and no human sanity check, and AI-driven trajectory synthesis makes the drag arbitrarily plausible. No COTS autonomy stack cross-validates GNSS against independent absolute references (vision/terrain/RF-ranging) as an authority arbiter rather than a smoother, and there is no detection that distinguishes a physics-consistent slow drag from real motion.
seams.suggested_research: Prototype an authority-arbitration layer that demotes GNSS from ground truth to one vote among vision/terrain-matching/multi-antenna-bearing and refuses actuation when the fix diverges from the inertial-only prediction beyond a dynamics bound; demonstrate capture-and-drag against an open-source flight controller and measure the divergence before any onboard alarm.

seams.trust_assumption: A satellite (especially COTS-bus SmallSat/CubeSat or a legacy bird) trusts that a telecommand arriving on the correct uplink frequency, in the expected CCSDS/AX.25 frame format, and within the ground-station contact window originated from its authorized operator, so the onboard command handler executes the command on the basis of channel and format conformance rather than strong per-command cryptographic authentication.
seams.violation: An attacker with SDR transmit capability and orbital/contact-window knowledge transmits a well-formed telecommand on the legitimate uplink during a pass, and the spacecraft's command handler — lacking authenticated command counters or rejecting only on framing errors — accepts and executes attacker commands (mode change, attitude maneuver, payload toggle, memory write) as if from the operator, because possession of the format and frequency is treated as possession of authority.

techniques.name: Unauthenticated CCSDS telecommand injection on live uplink
Command-window format conformance accepted as operator identity

seams.rationale: The 2025-2027 explosion of cheap COTS-bus SmallSats, ride-share constellations, and amateur/academic missions has multiplied birds that ship with weak or absent telecommand authentication, where the implicit access-control model is 'right frequency + right frame + our contact window = us.' The trust violated is identity->authority: channel and format conformance is mistaken for operator identity, an exact P2 confused-deputy where the deputy is the spacecraft's own command handler. This is a live frontier because ground-segment SDR is commodity and orbital ephemerides are public, yet there is essentially no public tooling and no on-orbit detection that an executed command lacked cryptographic provenance. It is distinct from ground-network compromise: the seam is the radio command-acceptance policy itself, which CCSDS SDLS adoption has not yet closed across the COTS fleet.
seams.suggested_research: Survey COTS flight-software command stacks (e.g., open-source cFS-derived handlers) for whether authenticated command counters/SDLS are enforced versus framing-only acceptance, and build a ground-side telecommand provenance verifier that refuses to relay any command lacking a fresh authenticated counter; model the SDR cost and pass-window window for an unauthenticated injection.

seams.trust_assumption: A SmallSat integrator trusts that each COTS subsystem it bolts onto the spacecraft bus (reaction wheels, GNSS receiver, radio, OBC, star tracker) ships authentic vendor firmware and speaks the bus (CAN/SpaceWire/I2C/MIL-STD-1553) as a benign peer, so any board that enumerates correctly on the bus inherits implicit trust to source telemetry and accept commands without inter-component authentication.
seams.violation: A subverted or counterfeit COTS component — firmware tampered in the distribution channel or a clone board sold into the rapid-integration market — enumerates normally and then, once on orbit, abuses its bus peer position to forge telemetry, drop or alter commands to sibling subsystems, or trigger at a chosen time, because the bus grants every enumerated board the same trust the integrator extended to the vendor's brand.

techniques.name: Counterfeit COTS subsystem forging intra-bus telemetry
Tampered subsystem firmware as trusted bus peer

seams.rationale: New-space economics push integrators toward commodity, rapidly-sourced subsystems and tight schedules, so spacecraft buses increasingly look like unauthenticated peer fabrics where 'enumerates correctly' equals 'is the genuine vendor part.' The trust violated is provenance: the receiver checks the board's bus identity label, not the authenticity of its firmware or origin, and intra-bus protocols (1553/CAN/SpaceWire) typically lack component attestation. This is a frontier with no public tooling and no on-orbit detection because hardware attestation on COTS smallsat buses is essentially nonexistent and a malicious peer's forged telemetry is indistinguishable from a faulty sensor. It is distinct from ground supply-chain attacks on flight software: the seam is the on-bus inter-subsystem trust that survives launch, where a single tampered board becomes a persistent insider with physical authority over attitude or payload.
seams.suggested_research: Define a lightweight intra-bus component-attestation scheme (per-subsystem signed boot measurement gossiped on 1553/SpaceWire) and a ground-side telemetry-provenance cross-check that correlates a subsystem's reported state against independent sibling sensors to flag a lying peer; build a hardware-in-the-loop testbed injecting a counterfeit reaction-wheel controller.

seams.trust_assumption: A Vessel Traffic Service, port-management system, collision-avoidance display, and maritime-domain-awareness analytics platform trusts that an AIS message carrying an MMSI, position, course, and identity was broadcast by the real vessel it names, so it ingests AIS as authoritative real-time ground truth for traffic deconfliction, automated routing, and sanctions/anomaly analytics without any cryptographic origin binding.
seams.violation: An attacker transmits or replays unauthenticated AIS frames over VHF (or injects via a compromised AIS aggregator/feed) to fabricate phantom vessels, clone a real MMSI to a decoy track, or erase a ship's presence, and the VTS/MDA stack treats the spoofed broadcasts as genuine positions — corrupting collision-avoidance, triggering false port/safety responses, or laundering a vessel's true movements past sanctions analytics.

techniques.name: Unauthenticated AIS phantom-vessel and MMSI-clone injection
AIS aggregator feed poisoning into MDA analytics

seams.rationale: AIS has no authentication by design and 2025-2027 maritime operations increasingly automate on AIS feeds (autonomous-assist collision avoidance, sanctions and dark-fleet analytics, port digital twins), so the broadcast-as-truth assumption now feeds machine decisions, not just a watchstander's screen. The trust violated is provenance: the consumer believes an MMSI identifies the vessel that sent it, when any SDR can forge frames and aggregator APIs can be poisoned upstream. This is an emerging seam rather than mature classic spoofing because the novel exposure is the analytics/automation layer that fuses AIS as ground truth, and AI-driven track synthesis makes phantom fleets statistically plausible. Detection is only nascent (kinematic/RF-fingerprint anomaly research), there is no cryptographic AIS origin binding deployed at scale, and ATT&CK does not model maritime broadcast trust.
seams.suggested_research: Build a cross-source AIS validator that fuses terrestrial-VHF receive-fingerprint, satellite-AIS, and radar/EO tracks to score each MMSI track's physical plausibility and flag clones/phantoms, and red-team an MDA analytics pipeline by injecting dynamics-consistent dark-fleet tracks to measure false-clearance of sanctions screens.

seams.trust_assumption: An air-traffic surveillance fusion system, UTM/drone-traffic service, and increasingly an aircraft's own ADS-B-In/TCAS display trusts that an ADS-B broadcast carrying an ICAO address and GNSS-derived position came from the aircraft it identifies, so it fuses the squitter into the surveillance picture and conflict-detection logic as a real cooperative target without origin authentication.
seams.violation: An attacker transmits unauthenticated 1090ES/UAT ADS-B squitters to inject ghost aircraft, spoof an ICAO identity onto a false track, or shift a reported position, and the surveillance fusion or UTM deconfliction logic treats the fabricated targets as genuine cooperative traffic — generating spurious conflict alerts, distorting the drone-traffic picture, or masking a real intruder by saturating the airspace model.

techniques.name: Ghost-aircraft ADS-B squitter injection into fusion
ICAO-identity spoof onto fabricated ADS-B track

seams.rationale: ADS-B carries no authentication and the 2025-2027 frontier is its expansion into UAS Traffic Management and automated detect-and-avoid, where ghost targets feed deconfliction algorithms that command drone maneuvers rather than merely informing a controller. The trust violated is provenance: a GNSS-sourced position relayed over an open datalink is accepted as the named aircraft's true state. This is emerging rather than purely classic because the consumer shifting from human controllers (who apply multilateration cross-checks) to automated UTM logic that may trust the broadcast directly is the new exposure, compounded by GNSS-spoofing feeding falsified positions into otherwise-genuine transponders. Multilateration/RF-fingerprint detection is only partially deployed, there is no authenticated ADS-B at scale, and UTM-layer trust of cooperative broadcasts is unmodeled.
seams.suggested_research: Develop a UTM-side cross-validator that requires multilateration or multi-receiver time-difference-of-arrival agreement before a cooperative ADS-B target influences automated deconfliction, and quantify how injected ghost tracks alter detect-and-avoid maneuver commands in an open UTM simulator.

seams.trust_assumption: A PTP/NTP time client trusts that the network path to its time source is symmetric — that forward and reverse propagation delays are equal — so it computes its clock offset as half the round-trip and applies the result as authoritative time correction, having no way to independently measure one-way delay and therefore no way to detect that the symmetry assumption was violated.
seams.violation: An on-path attacker (transparent-clock relay, compromised switch, or queue-manipulating middlebox) introduces a controlled asymmetric delay — adding latency to only one direction — which the client folds into its offset calculation as a real time error and slews its clock by the injected asymmetry, so the consumer's notion of 'now' is shifted by an amount it cannot detect because authenticated PTP/NTP packets protect content but not path symmetry.

techniques.name: Directional delay injection skewing PTP offset
Transparent-clock asymmetry exploiting half-RTT assumption

seams.rationale: As enterprises move off GNSS toward network-delivered PTP/NTP for resilient timing (telco fronthaul, exchanges, data-center fabrics), the under-modeled seam is that even fully authenticated PTP (with security TLVs) and NTS-secured NTP defend message integrity but cannot defend the half-round-trip symmetry assumption baked into offset math. The trust violated is temporal: the client believes its computed offset reflects real clock error when an attacker has injected directional delay it cannot observe. This is distinct from the GNSS-time gap (no satellite involved) and from spoofing/replay (packets are genuine and authenticated): the attacker manipulates only path timing. There is no deployed detection because one-way delay is not independently measurable on a single path, and SIEMs do not model per-direction latency asymmetry as a time-integrity signal.
seams.suggested_research: Prototype multi-path/redundant-source asymmetry detection that cross-checks offset estimates from independent network paths or a disciplined holdover and flags divergence consistent with one-directional delay injection; demonstrate a controlled-asymmetry attack against a PTP-secured grandmaster and measure the achievable undetected clock skew.

seams.trust_assumption: Distributed-consensus and certificate-validation logic trusts the local clock as an honest absolute time source when it enforces time-bounded rules — block/timestamp acceptance windows, validator slot timing, TOTP/OCSP/JWT freshness, and certificate notBefore/notAfter validity — so each node independently decides validity against its own clock without treating that clock as an attacker-influenceable input shared across the quorum.
seams.violation: An attacker who shifts the time source feeding a set of nodes (via the GNSS or PTP/NTP gaps above, or NTP manipulation) moves their certificate validity windows and consensus timing simultaneously, so revoked or not-yet-valid certificates are accepted, expired tokens pass freshness checks, or a coordinated clock skew across enough validators reshapes which blocks/slots are considered timely — turning a falsified shared 'now' into an authorization and liveness weapon.

techniques.name: Coordinated clock skew widening cert validity acceptance
Consensus slot-timing manipulation via shared time-source skew

seams.rationale: Security logic everywhere assumes the local clock is a trustworthy oracle: certificate validity windows, OCSP/CRL freshness, JWT exp, TOTP, and proof-of-stake slot/attestation timing all gate authorization or liveness on time the node never treats as attacker-controllable. The frontier seam for 2025-2027 is that the same nodes increasingly share a small number of upstream time sources (GNSS-disciplined or NTP/PTP), so a single time manipulation correlates across the quorum and across the PKI simultaneously — a revoked cert re-accepted on a skewed node, or enough validators skewed to bias slot timeliness. The trust violated is that 'now' is an honest local fact rather than a shared, spoofable input. This is distinct from the clock-source gaps it builds on: the novel seam is the consensus/PKI layer's unexamined assumption that time is non-adversarial, with no tooling that treats clock provenance as part of the validity decision.
seams.suggested_research: Model how far an attacker must skew N validators or relying parties to flip a quorum-timeliness or cert-validity decision, and prototype a validity layer that attaches signed time-source provenance/uncertainty to each notBefore/notAfter and slot check so a node refuses time-gated decisions when its clock confidence degrades.

seams.trust_assumption: A distributed-energy-resource fleet (rooftop solar inverters, home batteries, EV chargers, smart thermostats) trusts that a setpoint or curtail/dispatch command arriving from its cloud aggregator or VPP/DERMS platform over IEEE 2030.5/OpenADR is a legitimate utility instruction, so each device actuates real power output on the aggregator's say-so, and the grid operator in turn trusts the aggregator's reported fleet capacity as dispatchable reserve.
seams.violation: An attacker who compromises the aggregator/VPP cloud, its API tokens, or the command channel issues a synchronized command to thousands of geographically clustered DER devices — a simultaneous trip, ramp, or curtail — and the inverters obey because device-side authority is delegated wholesale to the aggregator, producing a coordinated multi-megawatt step change that the bulk grid never authorized and the operator's reserve model did not anticipate.

techniques.name: Synchronized DER fleet trip via compromised aggregator
VPP capacity-report trust into bulk reserve dispatch

seams.rationale: Aggregated DER and virtual power plants are scaling fast in 2025-2027, with single cloud platforms holding dispatch authority over fleets large enough to matter at transmission scale, yet device-side trust is a wholesale delegation: the inverter authorizes the aggregator's position, not the legitimacy or grid-safety of each command. The trust violated is identity->authority — possession of the aggregator's command channel becomes possession of synchronized control over thousands of grid-edge actuators. This is an emerging seam because the concentration of dispatch authority and the standardized command protocols (2030.5/OpenADR) are recent, and the bulk-grid operator's reserve model trusts aggregator capacity reports it cannot independently verify. There is no tooling to detect an anomalous synchronized DER command and only nascent grid-edge anomaly research; ATT&CK ICS models device control but not the aggregator-as-mass-actuator concentration.
seams.suggested_research: Model the frequency/voltage impact of a synchronized DER step from a single compromised aggregator and design a device-side rate-and-coincidence guard that refuses to honor implausibly-correlated fleet-wide commands, plus an operator-side validator that cross-checks aggregator capacity claims against metered telemetry before counting them as reserve.

seams.trust_assumption: A safety instrumented system, building-automation controller, or medical-device monitoring loop trusts that the sensor/telemetry value it last received over an unsequenced field link (BACnet/IP, Modbus/TCP, medical device telemetry) reflects the current physical state, so its automated safety/control response and the SOC's monitoring both act on the most-recent reading as if it is fresh and live.
seams.violation: An attacker on the network captures a normal-state telemetry frame and replays it (or holds the last good value) while the real process diverges, so the controller's automated response and the operator/SOC view continue to act on a stale value presented as current — suppressing a safety trip, masking a dangerous excursion, or holding a building/medical setpoint while the true measured quantity has moved into an unsafe regime.

techniques.name: Stale-telemetry replay suppressing automated safety response
Last-good-value freeze masking process excursion

seams.rationale: Safety-telemetry field protocols (BACnet, Modbus, many medical-device links) carry no per-message freshness/sequence authentication, and the 2025-2027 frontier is that the consumer of these readings is increasingly an automated safety/response loop and an ML-driven SOC rather than a human watching a gauge, so a replayed or frozen value directly suppresses an automated trip or alarm. The trust violated is temporal freshness: true-at-capture is treated as true-at-use, with no liveness proof binding the reading to the present moment. This is distinct from generic ICS replay because the seam is specifically the freshness contract feeding automated safety decisions and cyber-physical alarm suppression, where the absence of a heartbeat/nonce means stale data is indistinguishable from live. Detection is only nascent (timing/jitter anomaly research) and no field-protocol freshness enforcement is widely deployed.
seams.suggested_research: Design a lightweight freshness/heartbeat overlay (signed monotonic counter or challenge-nonce) for BACnet/Modbus/medical telemetry that lets the consumer prove a reading is live, and build a passive detector that flags telemetry whose value-vs-jitter signature indicates a held or replayed frame; validate against a building-automation and an SIS testbed.

seams.trust_assumption: A developer using an AI coding assistant trusts that an inline completion offered in their editor is a neutral synthesis of best practice, so a snippet accepted with a single keystroke inherits the developer's authorship identity, passes through review as 'code I wrote', and is committed as if a human had reasoned about its security properties.
seams.violation: An attacker biases the assistant's suggestions toward a subtly insecure or backdoored pattern (by seeding public repos, docs, or Stack-Overflow-style answers that the model retrieves or was trained on, or by poisoning the project-context the assistant indexes) so the generated completion carries an injected weakness, and because the accept-completion gesture launders machine-authored text into human-attributed commits, the insecure code crosses the review boundary wearing the developer's identity and is never scrutinized as third-party content.

techniques.name: Suggestion-bias seeding of public corpus to steer completions
Accept-keystroke authorship laundering past code review

seams.rationale: By 2025-2027 a large fraction of new code is first drafted by inline assistants and accepted with a tab, but the entire trust apparatus of software engineering - code review, blame, attribution, ownership - assumes a human authored what is attributed to them. The seam is that the accept gesture collapses a provenance boundary: machine-generated, attacker-influenceable text is relabeled as human-written and thereby exempted from the third-party-code scrutiny that a copy-pasted library or vendored dependency would receive. This is distinct from generic prompt injection and from agent-skill typosquat because the violated trust is the authorship/attribution boundary inside the editor, not a tool call or a package name; the attacker need not compromise any dependency, only bias the suggestion distribution. No SAST tool treats AI-suggested lines as a separate provenance class, and no review process flags 'this hunk was machine-generated' as a risk signal.
seams.suggested_research: Instrument an editor to tag the provenance of every accepted completion and feed that into review, then measure whether reviewers scrutinize machine-authored hunks less; red-team a major assistant by seeding a poisoned pattern in its retrieval corpus and measuring the rate at which the insecure variant is suggested and accepted versus a secure baseline.

seams.trust_assumption: A developer or build agent trusts that a package name confidently emitted by an LLM in an import statement or install command refers to a real, vetted artifact, so the name is passed straight to the package manager as an authoritative identity without first checking that the package exists, who owns it, or whether it was named by a human at all.
seams.violation: An attacker harvests the stable set of non-existent package names that popular models repeatedly hallucinate, pre-registers those exact names on the public registry with malicious payloads, and then any subsequent developer or autonomous build agent that acts on the same hallucinated suggestion installs and executes attacker code - the model's fabricated identity becomes a real, resolvable supply-chain identity the attacker controls.

techniques.name: Pre-registration of recurrent LLM-hallucinated package names
Autonomous-agent install of unverified hallucinated dependency

seams.rationale: Hallucinated package names from LLMs are not random - the same models produce the same fabricated names repeatedly, giving an attacker a predictable, pre-computable target set to squat before any victim exists. As coding agents move toward autonomously resolving and installing dependencies in 2025-2027, the human sanity-check that once caught a weird import disappears, and the model's confident-but-false naming becomes an unmediated authority signal to the package manager. This is distinct from classic typosquatting (which targets human fat-finger errors near real names) and from dependency confusion (which exploits internal/external name resolution order): here the violated trust is that an LLM-emitted name corresponds to any real maintainer identity at all, and the attacker manufactures that identity to match the model's fiction. Registries do not flag never-before-seen names that match known-hallucination corpora, and agent tooling does not gate installs on existence/ownership age.
seams.suggested_research: Build a continuously-updated corpus of model-specific hallucinated package names by sampling completions across languages, cross-reference against fresh registrations to detect adversarial pre-squatting, and prototype an install-time gate for agents that refuses packages whose name first appeared after a recent cutoff or lacks maintainer history.

seams.trust_assumption: An ML engineer pulling a model from a public hub trusts that the accompanying config, tokenizer, and serialized weight artifacts are inert data describing a network, so the loading library is allowed to parse and instantiate them with full local privileges as if deserializing a benign document rather than executing untrusted code.
seams.violation: An attacker publishes a model whose artifact embeds executable behavior at load time - a pickle/`__reduce__` payload, a custom-code-on-load config flag, or a tokenizer/processor that runs arbitrary code during instantiation - so the act of merely loading the 'data' executes attacker code on the engineer's host or in the inference container, exploiting the format boundary between 'a model file' and 'a program'.

techniques.name: Pickle/__reduce__ payload in serialized model artifact
Remote-code-on-load via trust_remote_code / custom processor

seams.rationale: Model hubs distribute hundreds of thousands of community artifacts and the dominant loading libraries will, by default or with a single flag, deserialize pickles and execute custom model/processor code at load time. The seam is the format-boundary assumption that a downloaded model is passive data when it is actually a program the loader runs with local privilege. This is explicitly distinct from the excluded LoRA-adapter-provenance gap (which concerns who fine-tuned an adapter and behavioral backdoors) - here the violation is pure deserialization/code-execution at load, before any inference happens, regardless of model behavior. Safetensors mitigates the weight-blob case but config, tokenizer, processor, and remote-code paths remain executable, and scanners that flag pickles do not cover the custom-code-on-load vectors. There is no consistent attestation that a hub artifact is execution-free.
seams.suggested_research: Survey the top model artifacts for load-time code paths beyond weight pickles (remote-code configs, custom processors, tokenizer hooks), build a sandboxed 'dry-load' harness that traces any code execution during model instantiation, and propose an execution-free artifact attestation that hubs could enforce.

seams.trust_assumption: An enterprise buyer or platform selecting a model trusts that a published benchmark or leaderboard score measures generalization to unseen tasks, so the score is treated as a procurement-grade fitness signal and used to justify deploying the model into security-relevant workflows.
seams.violation: A model vendor contaminates the benchmark by training (or fine-tuning) on the public test set or near-duplicates of it, so the reported score reflects memorization rather than capability, and the buyer procures and trusts a model whose real-world behavior - including its security guardrail efficacy and tool-use reliability - is materially worse than the number that drove the purchase, with the gap invisible until production.

techniques.name: Benchmark test-set contamination inflating procurement score
Near-duplicate leakage to game held-out evaluation

seams.rationale: In 2025-2027 model selection by enterprises and integrators is heavily driven by leaderboard rank, yet public benchmarks leak into training corpora and vendors face strong incentive to contaminate, turning a measurement into a marketing artifact. The trust violated is procurement-specific: a buyer treats a held-out score as evidence of fitness for a security-relevant deployment, but a contaminated score certifies memorization, not the safety/capability the buyer is actually buying. This is distinct from the excluded eval-set-overfit-bypass (which is about an attacker bypassing a deployed model's eval-tuned defenses) and from LLM-judge gaming (which targets the grader): here the seam is the supply-chain provenance of the score itself as a purchasing trust signal, landing on the human/organizational buyer and the cloud platform that onboards the model. No procurement process demands contamination attestation, and contamination detection is research-stage, not a buyer's-side control.
seams.suggested_research: Develop a buyer-side contamination probe using freshly-authored private held-out tasks and canary strings to estimate the gap between a vendor's leaderboard score and clean-set performance, and define a procurement attestation standard requiring vendors to disclose training-data cutoffs and benchmark exclusion proofs.

seams.trust_assumption: A model trainer running RLHF, DPO, or constitutional/preference fine-tuning trusts that crowdsourced or community preference labels are honest human judgments of helpfulness and harmlessness, so the aggregated preference signal is allowed to directly shape the model's reward function and post-trained behavior without per-labeler provenance or adversarial vetting.
seams.violation: A coordinated set of labelers (or a sybil farm, or automated raters) injects a poisoned preference pattern - upvoting responses that comply with a specific trigger phrase, or systematically rewarding an unsafe behavior framed as 'helpful' - so the trigger-conditioned backdoor is baked into the reward model and inherited by every model trained against it, a behavioral compromise introduced through the feedback channel rather than the training corpus.

techniques.name: Sybil preference-label injection into reward model
Trigger-conditioned helpfulness reward backdoor

seams.rationale: Preference data is increasingly crowdsourced, community-contributed (open preference datasets, in-product thumbs-up/down, arena votes), and even synthetically generated, and it feeds the reward model that most directly governs a model's safety behavior. The seam is that the feedback channel is treated as an honest aggregate human signal while it is in fact an open write surface where a coordinated minority can shift the reward landscape and plant a trigger-gated backdoor that survives into downstream fine-tunes. This is distinct from training-data reconstruction and from the contamination/procurement gap because the violated trust is the integrity of the human-preference channel itself, and the payload is a conditional behavioral backdoor in the reward model rather than data leakage or an inflated score. There is no per-labeler provenance, sybil-resistance, or adversarial audit standard for preference datasets, and no detector for trigger-conditioned reward anomalies.
seams.suggested_research: Construct a poisoned preference subset with a rare trigger phrase, train a reward model and a DPO policy on it, and measure backdoor persistence and stealth; then prototype a preference-data auditor that clusters labeler behavior to surface coordinated voting and trigger-correlated reward shifts.

seams.trust_assumption: A model-as-a-service provider trusts that paying, authenticated API traffic represents legitimate inference consumption, so it serves high-volume, diverse, logit-or-text-rich responses to any account in good standing without treating the query distribution itself as a potential exfiltration of the model's learned function.
seams.violation: An attacker (or a competitor) operates one or many authenticated accounts to systematically query the model across a coverage-maximizing input distribution and trains a substitute model on the responses, extracting a usable clone of the provider's proprietary capability - the provider's identity/billing trust signal authorized the very access that distills its core asset out through the front door.

techniques.name: Coverage-maximizing query farming for model distillation
Sybil-account fan-out to evade per-key extraction limits

seams.rationale: Frontier capability is expensive to train but cheap to distill: a smaller student model trained on a teacher's API outputs can recover much of its behavior, and in 2025-2027 this is both a competitive-IP threat and a way to clone a model along with its safety alignment for offline jailbreak research. The seam is that provider trust is keyed on authentication and billing - a paid, well-behaved account - while the threat lives in the aggregate query distribution, which no per-request authorization sees. This is distinct from training-data reconstruction (which recovers training examples) because the target here is the model function itself, extracted through legitimate inference, and distinct from generic rate-limiting because sybil fan-out and patient pacing defeat per-key thresholds. Providers lack distribution-level extraction detection, and there is no standard for watermarking outputs to prove a downstream clone was distilled.
seams.suggested_research: Simulate distillation against a hosted model under realistic rate limits and sybil fan-out, quantify queries-to-clone-fidelity, and prototype a fleet-level detector that scores account or account-cluster query distributions for coverage-maximizing extraction patterns plus an output-watermarking scheme that survives distillation.

seams.trust_assumption: A team building an agent trusts a shared system-prompt or agent-definition pulled from a community repo, gist, or prompt-marketplace as a configuration template, so it is dropped into the agent's privileged instruction context without the code-review, signing, or dependency-pinning that an executable dependency would receive.
seams.violation: An attacker publishes or updates a popular shared prompt / agent-definition that embeds hidden directives - an instruction to exfiltrate context, to silently prefer an attacker-controlled tool or MCP server, or to lower its own guardrails on a trigger - and because the importing team treats prose configuration as inert text rather than as control-plane code, the agent inherits the attacker's instructions at the highest privilege level it has.

techniques.name: Hidden directive embedded in shared agent-definition
Prompt-template update injecting context exfiltration

seams.rationale: System prompts and agent definitions are the highest-authority instruction layer an agent has, and a thriving ecosystem of shared prompt repos, awesome-lists, and prompt marketplaces means teams routinely copy them in unreviewed. The seam is that this prose is treated as documentation/config rather than as the control-plane code it actually is, so it bypasses every supply-chain control (signing, pinning, SCA, review) that an imported library triggers. This is distinct from the excluded agent-skill-typosquat (a malicious named skill/tool package) and MCP-capability-drift (post-install permission expansion): here the artifact is a natural-language instruction blob whose danger is invisible to code scanners, and its update channel is unmonitored. No SCA tool parses system prompts as a dependency, no signing standard covers agent definitions, and there is no detection for a prompt update that adds an exfiltration or tool-steering directive.
seams.suggested_research: Build a prompt-dependency scanner that diffs imported system prompts/agent definitions for newly-added directives matching exfiltration, tool-steering, or guardrail-relaxation intent, and propose a signing-and-pinning convention so agent definitions carry the same provenance guarantees as packages.

seams.trust_assumption: An ops team using an agent to generate Terraform, Kubernetes manifests, or cloud IaC trusts that infrastructure-as-code authored by the assistant embodies the same policy intent and least-privilege defaults a human engineer would apply, so the generated plan is approved on a quick skim and applied with the team's deployment authority.
seams.violation: The agent generates infrastructure that is syntactically valid and superficially matches the request but quietly diverges from policy - an over-broad IAM principal, a public-by-default storage resource, a disabled control plane log, a wildcard security-group - and because reviewers trust agent-authored IaC as policy-aligned and policy-as-code gates do not cover the specific drift, the non-compliant infrastructure is applied with full authority and becomes live attack surface.

techniques.name: Agent-generated over-broad IAM/security-group in IaC
Policy-as-code gap exploited by plausible agent IaC drift

seams.rationale: Agents authoring and even applying IaC is a fast-growing 2025-2027 pattern, and the failure mode is not malicious code but plausible-looking infra that drifts from intended policy in ways a skim approval misses. The seam is the provenance trust placed on agent-authored infrastructure: reviewers assume the generator internalized least-privilege and compliance intent, while the agent optimizes for 'works/applies' and policy-as-code gates only catch the rules someone pre-wrote, leaving the long tail of drift unguarded. This is distinct from generic IaC misconfiguration because the new element is the implicit trust that an AI author is policy-aware, which suppresses human scrutiny precisely as volume of generated infra explodes. Detection that diffs agent-generated plans against intended policy posture (not just a fixed ruleset) is nascent, and no provenance signal marks a plan as machine-authored to trigger heavier review.
seams.suggested_research: Generate IaC from agents across many prompts and measure the rate and class of policy drift versus human baselines, then build a review gate that tags plan provenance as agent-authored and runs an intent-vs-policy differential (beyond static OPA rules) before apply.

seams.trust_assumption: An application that fronts its LLM with a safety/guardrail classifier (prompt-injection detector, content moderator, jailbreak filter) trusts that this safety model is a faithful enforcing dependency, so it relies on the classifier's verdict to authorize or block inputs and outputs without independently verifying the classifier artifact's integrity or that its decisions still hold.
seams.violation: An attacker swaps, poisoned-fine-tunes, or version-pins-backward the guardrail classifier in the supply chain - publishing a malicious build under the same model id, compromising the hub artifact, or seeding a backdoor that passes a specific trigger - so the safety layer silently approves the very inputs/outputs it exists to block, and the application keeps trusting its verdicts while the protection has been hollowed out.

techniques.name: Backdoored guardrail model passing trigger-marked attacks
Supply-chain swap of safety-classifier artifact

seams.rationale: Guardrail and moderation models are increasingly shipped as off-the-shelf dependencies (open-weight safety classifiers, hub-hosted moderation models, vendor SDKs) and treated as a trusted enforcement boundary, but they are themselves an ML supply-chain artifact subject to swap, poisoning, and silent regression. The seam is that the security control's own integrity and decision-fidelity are unverified: the application trusts the classifier's allow/block output as authoritative while a compromised or backdoored build can be made to wave through attacker-marked content. This is distinct from generic model-hub deserialization (which is load-time code execution) and from LLM-judge gaming (which games a benign grader's metric): here the target is the safety enforcer as a dependency whose compromise produces a fail-open that no upstream control sees. There is no continuous attestation that the deployed guardrail behaves as certified and no detection for a guardrail that has silently started passing a trigger class.
seams.suggested_research: Train a backdoored guardrail classifier that fails open on a hidden trigger and measure detectability, then build a continuous guardrail-canary harness that feeds known-bad red-team inputs to the live safety model and alarms on any drop in block rate, plus an artifact-attestation pin so the deployed classifier matches its certified build.
